videos.title: This is absolutely CRAZY
videos.transcript_text: I've joined Anthropic. This shook me when I first read it and I couldn't quite figure out why and I thought about it and now I want to explain exactly why I think this is such a big deal. Andre Carpathy, if you're not familiar, is one of the most influential people in artificial intelligence. He invented self-driving at Tesla. He was an open AI co-founder which makes this entire story even more wild because now he's joining anthropic. Also one of the best educators in artificial intelligence able to break down extremely complex topics in ways that almost anybody can learn it. A lot of people thought this was quite shocking because look it has 24 million views on basically what amounts to a job update. This is actually a sign of the times. The AI labs have become almost religions and based on his decision to join Anthropic, we have learned a lot about his view of the future and it's not positive. I think the next few years at the frontier of LLM will be especially formative. I am very excited to join the team here and get back to R&D research and development. I remain deeply passionate about education and plan to resume my work on it in time. On multiple measures, Anthropic is winning the AI race. They now have the most revenue. They seemingly have the strongest culture whether you agree with the culture or not. And we're going to get into that. They also seem to be attracting, as exampled by Karpathy, the best people in the world to join them. And maybe that's another reflection of their culture. But what is their culture and how does it compare to open AI? It just seems like we're in a complete era of dogma. when you join a company you are planting a flag and saying this is what I believe and so by Karpathy joining anthropic he's basically validating a lot of what anthropic has been saying and if you don't remember what anthropic has been saying it's quite pessimistic about the future for example the founder of anthropic Daario Amodai spent last year warning of an AI white collar bloodbath he also leads the company that believes artificial intelligence could be conscious and that they believe it has the highest likelihood of becoming sentient and not in the good way, in the Terminator way. And also that these models are dangerous and that open source was a bad idea and also that they were the only company that was responsible enough or thoughtful enough to actually have the models and it just seems like Karpathy has joined the empire. by the way, completely wild that he decided to drop this news during Google IO's keynote. The point of this video is it just seems like pessimism is taking over. And so although Andre Karpathy didn't directly say it, by joining Anthropic, he is effectively saying Anthropic is the only company that he believes has a direct line of sight and the right set of ingredients to succeed with artificial intelligence. And I also had a sense of loss as I said that we lost this independent voice, this researcher who was also able to educate so many people about artificial intelligence. But also maybe he just believed this stuff. Maybe he believed that there really wasn't a bright future. It made me sad to realize that. I think about when Peter Steinberger joined Open AI. Peter Steinberger the creator of Open Claw. I had a similar feeling at first. I felt a loss. There was this absolute genius who was creating something new and innovative and independent and open-source. And when he joined OpenAI, I felt we lost a part of that. And I'm feeling that again here. And it just seems like there are two and a half companies that are just taking all of the oxygen out of the room. All of the points of view, all of the perspectives are just getting sucked into basically two and a half companies. And which ones are those? Of course, Open AI, we have Anthropic, and the half is X AI. They have strong perspectives. They just don't quite have that frontier model yet. One of the most interesting parts of this story is the fact that Andre Karpathy is an OpenAI co-founder. He left a while ago, but he was one of the original people on the OpenAI team. He also was on Elon's team at Tesla. But the interesting part now is he's joining Anthropic, and that is, at least from my perspective, a big slap in the face to Sam Alman and the Open AI culture. He didn't believe in them enough to go with them, even though he started there. And that is so wild to me. There is just something going on at Anthropic that pulls the best people in and keeps them there. Not a single Anthropic founder has left in in all the years that they've been operating. There is something to be said about that. And so, let's talk about the different perspectives of these companies. I think we all know Anthropic. I've already talked about it. Very pessimistic. They believe they're the only ones holier than thou that can create the right kind of AI in the future. Then we have open AI and they believe AI is more of a tool and that it is going to create abundance and that there is going to be this a bit painful transition period but mostly they have an optimistic view of the future. And then we have XAI and Elon Musk and they are pretty explicitly optimistic about the future redoing their company's mission and Tesla's mission. Even though XAI and Tesla are separate companies, they're basically the same because it's all Elon that there's going to be amazing abundance in the future and we're going to have not just universal basic income but universal high income. This is these are all words that Elon said directly. And so imagine you're one of the best researchers on the planet. You could go work anywhere. You can command compensation of any amount. And really to Cararpathy, it doesn't even matter. The money doesn't matter. He's already a billionaire most likely, right? Cuz he's a co-founder of OpenAI. His decision to join Anthropic is extremely telling. It's either he believes that they are the only company that is able to create artificial intelligence in a safe way or maybe just at all. He also believes in their world view. Job loss is going to be very real and very impactful. And then maybe he just saw something internally. He's technical through and through. He's a researcher at heart. And so he might have just seen something inside of Anthropic that got him so excited that he wanted to go and lend his expertise to the company to help them drive towards that future. Now typically people in tech are motivated by two things obviously money but the mission and I know it sounds corny to a lot of people but the mission of these companies is generally quite important when you can go work anywhere work on anything the actual problem you're solving and the reason why you are solving that problem is incredibly important and so again this is quite telling why carpathy joined anthropic. And look, this is something I think about quite a bit. It seems like pessimism, negativity towards AI has completely taken over the narrative. I mean, look at this Pew Research study. Americans continue to be wary of AI's impact on daily life. 50% of Americans are more concerned than excited about the increased use of AI in daily life. But it's clear a ton of people are concerned and it has actually been increasing especially in 2023 which is when chatbt was released and it has also become politicized. Politics especially in the US are completely polarized. There is no middle ground. There is no gray area. You're either for something or you're against it and you are vehemently so. Of course because AI is the biggest thing right now. It has become politicized. Whether you're talking about Bernie Sanders having a conversation with Claude on which is just a phone on the other side of the table, which is like the most absurd image ever. Let's not forget this. When Bernie Sanders decided to interview Claude and it was on this this stand with I don't know. It's just such like a crazy image, right? Are we are we seeing this? I mean, the fact that he's interviewing Claude and he thought this was a legitimate interview is the craziest thing ever. You know, whatever you think about Bernie Sanders, fine. But it has become completely politicized. Bernie Sanders is talking about pausing AI completely. AOC is talking about pausing AI completely. And just so you don't think I'm completely favoring one side, the Trump administration with the Department of War went to war with anthropic. And so, of course, because it has become so politicized, it is binary in that it's either extremely good or extremely bad. Just admitting something from my channel. I have historically been extremely optimistic about artificial intelligence. Maybe to the point where some people saw me as naive. And we actually had meetings as a team to think through that. And I actually think that was quite valid feedback. If you're optimistic about everything, your opinion doesn't carry as much weight. And so I try to be critical when it is warranted. But the general sentiment around AI right now is just seemingly pure pessimism outside of a small bubble of AI insiders. Even within that bubble, anthropics stands alone as a paragon of pessimism. Is paragon the right word? By the way, this isn't new. The entire social media apparatus is based on optimizing for fear and anger. Facebook and Meta kind of knew that for a while and they didn't do anything about it and this was especially harmful to kids. They actually had to pay a lot of money because of that. Just a few months ago, New Mexico jury says meta harms children's mental health and safety. So, this is nothing new. The social media algorithms are optimizing for one thing and that is engagement time on site because that means they get to serve more ads. And of course, human nature is attracted to fear and anger. Those are the easy emotions to gravitate towards. And we're seeing the same thing now with AI. Politicians want to win races. So, they point to the big bad boogeyman that is AI. AI data centers are going to drink up all the water. They're going to take all your jobs. It doesn't really matter what it is. It's just AI bad. It's certainly not all sunshine and rainbows. It's somewhere in the middle. Think about 24-hour news built around keeping you engaged and keeping you watching for as long as possible. So, of course, them as well, optimizing for fear and anger. If all they did was tell you that everything is good and you should feel optimistic, you'd probably turn the TV off and go outside and touch grass. But instead, they have to draw you in with that fear and anger. And that's what I am deeply concerned about because AI has the potential to have truly a positive impact on the world. And if we see it as really a deeply negative thing or a deeply polarizing thing, then we're going to have a large portion of the population fearful of it, which is bad in itself. But they're going to be more willing to enact policy to stop AI or to slow down AI. And that actually puts us at risk because I guarantee other countries are not going to be slowing down when it comes to AI. And AI is one of the most powerful technologies and innovations ever created. So we do want it to be ours. And by the way, I'm not saying that the AI industry is not to blame for some of this pessimism. AI companies are doing themselves no favors when it comes to the perception of artificial intelligence. And a great example is the ex Google CEO Eric Schmidt getting absolutely booed at a commencement speech where he conveys how important AI will be to those graduating students. >> And it would this time it was the architects of artificial intelligence. Interesting. Every person and every relationship you have. I know what many of you are feeling about that. I can hear you. There is a fear. We do not know. We do not know. And if you if you'd let me make this point, please. If you don't care about science, that's okay because AI is going to touch everything else as well. Whatever path you choose, AI will become part of how work is done. If you have a pro problem in the world you want to solve, you can now assemble a team of AI agents to help you with the parts that you could never accomplish on your own. Let me give you some advice. First, find a way to say yes and thank you very much and good night. >> And Lulu Changy actually had a great take. And if you're not familiar with who she is, she's basically a PR comm's master. Question is not whether AI will shape the world. It will. The question is whether you will help. Tonedeaf disaster. Obvious move would have been to focus on the graduates as the protagonists instead of framing them as accessories to AI's world takeover. And this is truly the issue. Everything the AI industry talks about is this race to this nebulous idea of AGI, which nobody outside of a very small group of people actually knows what that means. And even that very small group of people disagree on what it means. And so what Eric Schmidt is effectively saying is get on board and help build this thing that most of you believe is going to replace you. It is not a compelling story to be telling kids that are literally sitting there about to graduate. And now a little bit on the personal front. When I think about making videos, the videos that are almost guaranteed to do well, the ones that are generally negative on some topic, when Anthropic does something kind of crappy with their pricing or they don't treat their developers well or they're not transparent, calling them out, those videos tend to do really well. But there's also a lot of positive things in the AI industry happening that also deserve to be highlighted. And I feel like I have this tension in the business where if I want to get more views, I should probably dunk on Google or I should dunk on anthropic. And there are plenty of YouTubers who do that. But I am actually quite optimistic about the future. And I need to balance being optimistic while also being critical where it is warranted. And I know a lot of you in the comments have actually said things about it. Especially in the early days of my videos, you would always say, "Oh, of course Matt is hyping this up or Matt is excited about this. He never has anything negative to say." And I I took that to heart. Again, that was very valid feedback because I was so excited and I didn't want to dunk on builders. I am a builder at heart. I did come from building startups and building companies and putting my absolute heart and soul into something and putting it out there and just to have people dunk on it was such a crappy feeling. And so I didn't want to do that. But then again, I came to realize there is plenty of room to be critical of companies and decisions they make while also painting an overall optimistic picture of the future. That is what I believe is also best to convey to everybody else. coming back to Andre Carpathy trying to figure out why I was so affected by him joining Anthropic and I realized that's the reason is because he is co-signing effectively everything anthropic has been saying that there's going to be complete job loss that they are the only company good enough to create artificial intelligence in a safe way that opens source doesn't deserve to exist. These are not things I believe in the slightest. And so I saw Andre Karpathy as this shining light of education and optimism. But maybe it just wasn't that. Or just to paint a nice little optimistic angle on it, maybe him joining will inject a bit of optimism into Anthropic. Let's put a bow on this. I want to be optimistic. I enjoy being optimistic. I think there's plenty of room to be critical and to criticize companies when they make bad decisions. And I'm going to try to strike a balance. Not always going to get it right. Always open to your feedback, of course. Drop it in the comments below. I do read that. And I just hope Anthropic becomes net more positive, net more optimistic with Andre Karpathy on the team now. And by the way, there are plenty of examples of Anthropic being extremely pessimistic, including an essay that they just put out saying, "We have 2 years before China takes over." videos.title: Vikings, Ragnar, Berserkers, Valhalla & the Warriors of the Viking Age | Lex Fridman Podcast #495
videos.transcript_text: - The Viking longships could average 70 to 120 miles a day. They could hit a place, raid it, drag off whoever they wanted, and get away before you could get your army there. That's just absolutely terrifying. - What do you think it felt like for Alcuin and the monks to see the Viking ships on the horizon? - Honestly, I think it's the end of the world, and I don't think they were wrong to think that. The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle says the night before Lindisfarne, the monks saw sheets of lightning in the sky in the shape of dragons, and this obviously meant to foreshadow the dragon ships coming up. But if you were brave, then you got taken to the House of the Dead, which was Valhalla. Every day you would fight, and whatever wounds you got would be magically healed that night, and then the next morning, you'd get up and do it again, so you're essentially practicing for Ragnarok, the final battle. You know, there's this poem by Tennyson, Ulysses, my favorite poem. I think it captures the Viking spirit. The last line of it is to strive, "To seek, to find, and not to yield." I think that's very much like the Viking, you know, my purpose holds, to sail beyond the baths of all the Western stars until I die. We may die, but I'm gonna do this, I'm not gonna yield. - The following is a conversation with Lars Brownworth, a historian and author of many excellent history books, including The Sea Wolves: A History of the Vikings and The Normans: From Raiders to Kings. He's also the host of two history podcast series. The first called 12 Byzantine Rulers: The History of the Byzantine Empire is one of the first, if not the first ever, history podcasts launched over 20 years ago in June, 2005. His second series, Norman Centuries, explores the remarkable rise of the Normans from Viking raiders to the rulers of kingdoms stretching from England to Sicily. In this conversation, we focus primarily on the Vikings, the seafaring Norse warriors and explorers who, over a period of just 300 years, reshaped the medieval world and the trajectory of Western civilization as we know it. This is a Lex Fridman podcast. To support it, please check out our sponsors in the description where you can also find links to contact me, ask questions, give feedback, and so on. And now, dear friends, here's Lars Brownworth. Your writing and podcasts take us from the Vikings to the Normans to Crusades, to the collapse of the East Roman Empire, also known as the Byzantine Empire. There's a thread, I think, that connects the Vikings through all of it, so let's start at the beginning. Let's start with the Vikings. So the age of the Vikings was intense and violent, as you write about, often dated from 793 AD to 1066 AD. It lasted less than three centuries. So the start is often dated to June 8th, 793. What happened on June 8th, 793? - In June of 793, a group of Vikings, probably originating from Norway, arrived at the Holy Island of Lindisfarne, which was a monastic community, and they essentially slaughtered everyone, burned a couple of buildings and grabbed everything that had any value and left. And that was the first Viking raid that came in force. And I do think Lindisfarne is a good beginning date because the terror that it brought really signified what was to come for the next two to three centuries. - So the word of it has spread. Like there's a bunch of accounts, like the monk Alcuin wrote about this event in a letter to King Ethelred of Northumbria, quote, "It is nearly 350 years that we and our fathers have inhabited this most lovely land, and never before has such terror appeared in Britain as we have now suffered from a pagan race, nor was it thought that such an inroad from the sea could be made." What made this race so psychologically devastating to this monk and to the many other monks on the island and then to all of Britain? - That's a great quote. Um, Alcuin was not just a regular scholar, he was Charlemagne's favorite scholar and he's largely responsible, as much as one person can be, for the Carolingian Renaissance that had done so much to elevate the early medieval world. In fact, the spaces we have, the punctuation we have, spaces between words are likely a result of Alcuin's work. He was an extremely literate man, and you can hear the terror creeping into that. Um, and part of that has to do with monastic communities, the Church and the- what they thought a monastic community was. So the Church was viewed as a sacred place. Everyone in Europe, everyone in quotes, is nominally Christian, and the Church is an area of safety. It's a literal ark from the troubles of the world that you can flee to. I believe there are even rules in England, for example, that if you had killed someone, you could flee to a church and the civil authorities were not allowed to enter for up to 40 days. So you could have sanctuary there. And to violate this would've been the worst possible offense you could have given, which is why, you know, Thomas Becket's murder is so, so horrible in England. And the monks had dedicated themselves to a life of studying the Bible, to copying scriptures to prayer, to removing themselves literally from the temptations of the world. And so they would seek monasteries that were remote, and the most remote locations you could find were islands in the North Atlantic, because it's just so difficult to get there.... So the ocean was considered a place of safety. Not sailing on the ocean, but these islands were literal havens of peace and security and closeness to God. And so the fact that the Vikings hit this place of all places you could hit was the worst, the most terrifying kind of offense against medieval sensibilities. - So there's a kind of line that you understand you don't cross. evidence.quote: ChatGPT for PowerPoint changes the way you build presentations.

videos.summary: Create and edit presentations faster in PowerPoint focuses on Model Releases. Key transcript signal: ChatGPT for PowerPoint changes the way you build presentations.
videos.title: Create and edit presentations faster in PowerPoint
videos.transcript_text: ChatGPT for PowerPoint changes the way you build presentations. It turns scattered company context into a streamlined workflow, helping you create an exec-ready, customer-facing presentation in a fraction of the time. When I open the ChatGPT window, I can start by organizing my thinking before I build a single slide. I can bring in context from connected apps like Google Drive, Microsoft 365, and internal communication channels. I can also upload files from my local machine. In this case, I'm pulling together messy meeting notes, account context, and other source material into one place. Once I send that over, ChatGPT recommends a detailed structure for the deck. It breaks the presentation down slide by slide, including suggested bullets, tables, formatting, and overall flow. I can quickly review the outline, make sure the direction is right, and then ask ChatGPT to build the deck. I can also give it additional guidance. In this case, I want to ensure it meets the standards executives expect. So I'm going to use a skill called Executive Readiness Quality Pass. This helps apply a quality bar, formatting standards, and consistent communication throughout my presentation. Now let's get to building. ChatGPT creates the presentation, building it slide by slide. It'll go through and apply the polish, and this is looking pretty good. So the first version gives me a strong starting point. But as I review it, I notice a few areas that I want to improve, including some of the positioning language and meta commentary that made its way into the deck. So I can keep working with ChatGPT right here in PowerPoint to refine the messaging and tighten the final version. You'll see those changes happen right in line to the existing deck. Modifying the slide content. Awesome. Very happy with the final result. ChatGPT for PowerPoint takes what used to be a slow, manual process and turns it into a guided workflow from raw inputs to a high-quality deliverable.

evidence.quote: Codex can now work more persistently towards your goals,

videos.summary: Run long tasks in Codex using goals focuses on Model Releases. Key transcript signal: Once Codex is up and running, you can continue to use steering to send additional messages, for example, to have Codex correct course.
videos.title: Run long tasks in Codex using goals
videos.transcript_text: Codex can now work more persistently towards your goals, whether it's for hours or even days. The goal functionality is now available in the Codex app, IDE extension, and the CLI. With it, you can specify a concrete goal to achieve, and Codex will continue to work until that goal has been met. Let me show you how it works. Start by typing /goal in your message composer to enable the goal mode. From there, we can define our goal for Codex. This will act as both the original message to have Codex start the task and as instructions to have it determine if the task has been completed. The best goals are those where it is clear whether Codex has actually achieved the goal or not. For example, by specifying a measurable target or having clear test criteria that have to pass. If you struggle writing the goal yourself, you can ask Codex to help. Either by using Plan Mode first before using the resulting plan as your implementation goal, or by just asking Codex to interview you before crafting the goal and then having it set the goal itself. Once Codex is up and running, you can continue to use steering to send additional messages, for example, to have Codex correct course. You can also start side chats to understand the work done so far without having to interrupt the main task. Because Codex might take hours or even days, you can also pause and resume your goal later on. This is helpful, for example, when you're about to head out of the office and your laptop is about to lose internet connection. Once you're ready to continue, you can resume the goal or even edit it if your goal has changed or if you want to give Codex more concrete guidance. Using goals in Codex is incredibly powerful. We've seen many people have Codex make progress on complicated tasks for upwards of 100 hours on a single goal. So give it a try.

videos.title: Workspace agents in ChatGPT: Admin and builder controls
videos.transcript_text: Workspace agents can run important workflows for your team around the clock. Let's take a look at this product feedback Intel agent which gathers important customer contacts and product feedback from a CRM, creates artifacts such as PRD briefs and slides and actionable tickets and tools like linear. Now, this agent is set up to provide these weekly updates in Slack on a schedule as you can see here. If we go into the Slack channel, you can see how the agent supports the product team by consolidating contacts across the tools they use and proactively sharing its analysis in this team's shared Slack channel. The agent can also respond with more context, incorporate feedback, and apply that feedback next time it runs due to its memory. So, given that this agent has access to a number of tools and apps, let's take a look behind the scenes at the controls that agent builders have. Agent builders are the ones who determine what the agent has access to and the actions it's allowed to take. They're able to toggle specific write and read actions and also set up additional action constraints using natural language much like the agent building process. So, here for this agent, I've set up an action constraint so that it can only send emails to openai.com recipients as the agent is handling sensitive customer and product information. And in ChatGPT Enterprise, admins can govern this across the workspace with powerful roles-based access controls. Admins determine who can build, publish, and what apps and actions agents can have access to. So, let's take a look at the setup for apps. I'll quickly run through the additional controls an admin has for your agent builders and their agents. So, if we take a look here at Gmail, which was the application we were just on, we can take a look at the additional admin controls as it relates to restricting access to certain applications based off a user's role, the ability to have further constraints on specific app parameters and actions as you can see here. And last of all, the ability to set up human in the loop confirmation flows so that chat GPT will prompt for user confirmation for actions that it deems consequential. So all in all, with Workspace Agents, teams move faster while IT and admins keep the right controls in place.

evidence.quote: kind of like OpenAI’s Sora. Not new.  But wait a second… it still somehow

videos.summary: NVIDIA's New AI Turns One Photo Into A World That Never Breaks focuses on Model Releases. Key transcript signal: Here,  the core generator is a diffusion transformer, kind of like OpenAI’s Sora.
videos.title: NVIDIA's New AI Turns One Photo Into A World That Never Breaks
videos.transcript_text: I can’t believe that we are getting  this for free. What is this? Well, hold on to your papers Fellow  Scholars because you take just one image, and it creates a 3D explorable world  out of it. Super cool. They call it Lyra 2.0. It sounds almost too good to be  true, and it often is. I’ll tell you why. I grew up in Budapest, and now I live in  southern Hungary in a different, beautiful city called Pécs. And whenever I visit Budapest,  I love to walk around the parts where I grew up, it is always an incredible feeling. And I’m  thinking that if we can even use research technology to deliver just a fraction  of that feeling, that is fantastic. Or, if you need just one image, you can take a  Street View image and it will create a video game world out of it. Drop in a robot and have it train  there safely and learn how to be a good robot. A different variant of this  concept is called Cosmos, it’s a bit different. It creates simulation  data for training robots and self-driving cars.I recently tried a self-driving car in San  Francisco, and it was incredible… even though only part of its training comes from simulated  data, that part is crucial. This is a testament to how important and useful simulations are. They  unlocks unexpected solutions for tough problems. But, not so fast. This isn’t so  easy because unfortunately…we have a big problem. These worlds break down. Also, wait a second, DeepMind also did this  earlier, didn’t they? Genie 3. An image goes in, a game comes out. It can even be a  drawing, painting, whatever you wish. So how is this different? Is  this the same thing? Well, no. Okay, let me try to explain. A bit more  than a year ago an amazing AI appeared that claimed to have watched 1,000,000 hours  of Minecraft videos and thus remade the very coarse Minecraft game for us. And the interesting  thing was this. We look at something, look away, look back and…whoops! Yes, you saw it  right. That just happened. When we ask, okay little AI, what was  there, it says: “I dunno”. It did not have object permanence.  Nearly every human toddler has object permanence. So it had very limited memory,  so long-term consistency was really hard. But then, Genie 3 took one image and  generated interactive worlds with multi-minute consistency. All this progress in  just a bit more than a year. That is…insane. However, this is still not that  practical because over a few minutes, it still forgets. What I want  to see is long term coherence. So what is the solution? How do we get a world  from a photo that doesn’t break down? Is that even possible? Dear Fellow Scholars, this is  Two Minute Papers with Dr. Károly Zsolnai-Fehér. Well, most of these techniques see 2D  pixels on a flat screen. No 3D geometry just a bunch of numbers for pixel colors. Here,  the core generator is a diffusion transformer, kind of like OpenAI’s Sora. Not new.  But wait a second… it still somehow always remembers. The worlds never break. In  this one, looking away and looking back will always give you back what you saw earlier.  That is possible for video games with 3D geometry made by artists. But…how is that  even possible with this kind of system? Well, that is the point…almost  exactly like that! You see, it is using a per-frame 3D geometry cache. Okay, so what does that mean? Well, it means that  it keeps a small 3D memory of the scene. In simpler words, it doesn’t remember the  whole world as is, it just remembers the scaffolding of the world. And then, it is  able to recreate the rest consistently. So when you look away, and look back, it  doesn’t make up something new from scratch, no. Instead, it thinks, wait, what  was there a moment ago? Got it! Now, it does not store the whole  scene as is, it has a depth map, something they call a downsampled point  cloud, and some camera movement info. That is fantastic. But it turns  out…that’s not quite fantastic enough. This depth map is not for the whole global  scene. Because if you try to fuse everything into one giant 3D world, it is done in a  way that errors accumulate over time. Tiny mistakes start piling up, and then over time,  it gets more and more corrupted. It is kind of like doing a photocopy of something.  And then, a photocopy of the photocopy, and then…you know how that goes. It just gets  lower and lower quality over each step. Not good. Okay, so now, what is the solution? Well, instead, it keeps a separate little 3D snapshot for each  view. Then later, when it comes back, it can ask: which earlier views saw this place best? And it  uses those as memory. That is an incredible idea. So, does that really work? The ablation  study reveals the answer. This is a good paper so it proposes a bunch of puzzle  pieces, and it doesn’t just batch them together into one block and say look! It  works! No. It tests every single new puzzle piece in isolation and tells us for each  one, how much they add to the picture. Now, if you stored the whole scene globally, style consistency would worsen a bit, and  camera control, oh my. That is a disaster. Can we see what that looks  like? This is a good paper, so the answer is…yes! Oh goodness.  If you do the global scene thing, it starts producing the wrong camera views. While  the full proposed technique is much closer to what it should show. It really shows that these  concepts work in practice. So this is why they propose remembering the scaffolding  of the scene per frame. So much better! Love it. Note that there is so much more in the  paper, we really just scratched the surface here. But, not even this technique  is perfect. Limitations. One. Static scenes only. No moving stuff. Two, it inherits flaws from its training  data. Namely, if you have a dataset that has photometric inconsistencies, it will  inherit that. What does that mean? Well, if you feed it data with different kinds of  lighting and exposure, it will also appear in its predictions. Of course it does, the  training data tells it how the world works, and it thinks that lighting and exposure can  change on a whim. Three, the 3D geometry that we get from it can contain artifacts and  these weird little floaters. Hmm..but why? The issue is that the generated views are not  perfectly consistent with each other, and when you try to reconstruct 3D from them, these small  inconsistencies can turn into floaters and noise. If you ask me, these are very typical  problems for a first, or in this case, second version of such a work. And it  is very typical that all three of these will be ironed out just one more paper down  the line. Remember, this is the First Law of Papers. Do not look at where we are, look at  where we will be two more papers down the line. So, finally, we take just one photo, and  get to create incredible digital worlds that don’t break down. We finally have it.  That is fantastic. And we get all of this, model and code for free? Yes! What a time to be alive! A great gift for us Fellow Scholars  and tinkerers. Thank you so much for this.
